title: Architectural Tradeoff Analysis
subtitle: C4 — There's no "best" — only what you optimise for
markdown:
## Why this matters

Junior engineers ask "what's the best way?" Senior engineers ask "what are we optimising for, and what are we willing to give up?" That single shift is what gets you out of code monkey territory and into being trusted with design decisions.

Every architectural choice — SQL vs NoSQL, monolith vs microservices, sync vs async, cache or no cache — is a *tradeoff*, not an answer. Pretending otherwise ("NoSQL is faster") is how teams paint themselves into corners they spend years escaping.

## The framework: pick 2 of 5

Software design has 5 forces that constantly compete:

1. **Performance** (latency, throughput)
2. **Consistency** (everyone sees the same answer at the same time)
3. **Availability** (the system stays up when parts fail)
4. **Simplicity** (humans can reason about it)
5. **Cost** (money + ops burden)

You can prioritise 2-3 of these well. The rest will suffer. This is the engineering work — choosing *which* to suffer, *consciously*.

## A worked example: payments vs analytics

Both are databases. Both store records. But:

- **Payments service** — must prioritise CONSISTENCY (a debit must match a credit) and AVAILABILITY (downtime = lost transactions). Performance is secondary; cost is whatever it has to be. → Postgres with synchronous replication. Simple, slow, correct.

- **Analytics dashboard** — must prioritise PERFORMANCE (query 100M rows fast) and COST (we'll have a lot of data). Consistency is fine eventually (yesterday's numbers don't need to be exact-to-the-second). → ClickHouse / BigQuery / DuckDB. Fast, cheap, eventually correct.

Same underlying need ("store records"), opposite optimal choice. **The right answer comes from the constraint, not the technology.**

## How to actually do this in a design review

When someone proposes architecture, ask three questions:

1. **"What are we optimising for?"** Force them to name 2-3 of the 5 forces.
2. **"What are we explicitly NOT optimising for?"** This is where the real conversation happens. If they can't answer, they haven't thought it through.
3. **"What does the failure mode look like?"** Every design has a failure mode. The question isn't "will it fail" but "how, and what then."

If the proposer can answer all three crisply, approve. If they hand-wave, send them back. This is how senior engineers protect codebases from cargo-cult architecture.

## The honest take

Most "architectural debates" online are people optimising for *different things* and not realising it. Microservices vs monolith only makes sense once you've named what you care about. Without that frame, the debate is religious.

> **The principle:** Naming what you give up is the senior engineer's job. Naming what you want is the junior engineer's job. Promotion is when you start doing both.

diagrams.caption: The 5 forces — pick 2-3 to prioritise consciously
diagrams.source:
graph TD
    Constraint[What are we optimising for] --> P[Performance]
    Constraint --> C[Consistency]
    Constraint --> A[Availability]
    Constraint --> S[Simplicity]
    Constraint --> M[Money or Cost]
    P -.->|tradeoff| C
    C -.->|tradeoff| A
    P -.->|tradeoff| S
    A -.->|tradeoff| M
    P -.->|tradeoff| M
    style Constraint fill:#fde68a
    style P fill:#dbeafe
    style C fill:#dbeafe
    style A fill:#dbeafe
    style S fill:#dbeafe
    style M fill:#dbeafe

tutorOpener: Pick a recent technical decision you (or your team) made — say, choosing a database. What did you optimise for, and what did you knowingly give up?
